label: ai
topic: A father with Parkinson's collapses the night of a rocket launch
text:
The spine is a gantry. A scaffold built to hold the fragile vessel of the self against the crushing pull of the earth. But my father's gantry is failing. I have flown back to Florida because of the third surgery. His lumbar is a zipper of fresh staples, braced by titanium rods that show up on the X-rays like glowing white scaffolding. He has Parkinson's, a disease of subtraction. It takes the dopamine. It takes the balance. It takes the voice, thinning it down to a reedy whisper.

Tonight, we sit on the screened-in porch. The humid air smells of sulfur and blooming jasmine. We are not talking about the walker resting against the screen door. We are not talking about the catheter, or the pill sorter with its colorful plastic compartments marking the days he has left. We are talking about space.

Down the coast, at Cape Canaveral, a Falcon 9 is scheduled to launch. We are waiting for the sky to ignite.

"Escape velocity," my father says, his left hand resting on his thigh, beating a frantic, involuntary 4/4 rhythm against the denim. The tremor is always worse at night. "Twenty-five thousand miles per hour. That's what it takes to break orbit."

I look at him in the dim light. His shoulders are stooped, heavy under the invisible weight that presses him toward the floorboards. Gravity is the ultimate enemy. It is the enemy of the rocket, plotting to pull the tonnage of metal back into the sea. And it is the enemy of the Parkinson's patient, dragging the chin to the chest, the feet to the carpet, turning the body into a sinking ship.

"Do you think they're nervous?" I ask, staring at the black horizon.

"The astronauts?" He smiles, a slow, asymmetric shifting of his cheek. "They're strapped to a controlled bomb. Of course they're nervous. But they want to float. They want to know what it feels like to be weightless."

At 10:14 PM, the sky catches fire. It begins as a false dawn, an electric orange flare blooming over the tree line. Then comes the sound, a low, tectonic rumble that shakes the iced tea in our glasses. We watch the streak of fire arc upward, piercing the atmosphere, a triumphant spear of human engineering defying the earth. He watches it until the orange dot vanishes into the stars. For three minutes, he stops shaking. He is up there with them, unburdened, floating in the dark.

"Beautiful," he whispers.

We stand to go inside. I shadow him, my hands hovering inches from his waist. He shuffles over the threshold, his slippers dragging on the linoleum of the kitchen. The refrigerator hums.

It happens in a fraction of a second. The signal from his brain misfires, lost in the frayed wiring of his basal ganglia. His knees unhinge. The titanium in his back cannot hold the sudden dead weight.

He falls. It is not a graceful descent. It is the catastrophic failure of thrusters. I lunge, catching his arm, but his momentum pulls us both down. We hit the kitchen floor hard, my knee slamming into the cabinet, his breath rushing out in a sharp, pained hiss.

I scramble to hold him, my arms wrapping around his frail, trembling ribcage. We are pinned to the cheap linoleum, tangled together, trapped entirely by the devastating, inescapable gravity of the earth.
